If your warehouse cost spiked 3× last month, where do you start looking — credits, cloud services compute, or storage?

bigquery.blurb: Google's externalized Dremel — pure serverless SQL, with no warehouses to provision. Storage is Capacitor (a column format that pre-computes layout for typical access patterns) on Colossus; execution is shuffled across a shared slot pool, billed per-byte-scanned or per-slot-second. BI Engine adds in-memory acceleration, BigQuery ML pushes models inline, and BigLake + Iceberg open up the storage. Strong opinion: the lack of provisionable compute is BigQuery's biggest feature for analysts and biggest constraint for streaming-first teams.
questions: What does Capacitor encode that Parquet doesn't, and why was it built before Parquet existed?
How does Dremel's tree-shaped execution differ from a Spark stage DAG?
Why is BigQuery serverless not actually serverless under the hood — what does the slot pool look like?
What is the difference between flat-rate and on-demand billing, and when does each break even?

dbt.blurb: Tristan Handy's wedge that turned 'SQL files in a folder' into the analytics-engineering profession. dbt-core compiles Jinja-templated SELECT statements into a DAG, runs them on the warehouse (Snowflake/BigQuery/Databricks/Redshift/etc.), and adds tests, docs, and lineage on top. dbt Cloud sells orchestration, IDE, and Semantic Layer (post-Transform acquisition). Underneath everything: the warehouse owns the compute, dbt only owns the metadata + DAG layer.
questions: Why is dbt fundamentally a metadata + Jinja layer with no execution engine of its own?

confluent.blurb: Founded by Kreps, Narkhede and Rao — the Kafka authors at LinkedIn. Confluent took the open-source broker and added the parts enterprises actually pay for: Schema Registry to enforce contracts, Connect for source/sink plumbing, ksqlDB and now Flink for stream processing, Tiered Storage to push cold data onto S3, and KRaft to retire ZooKeeper. The 2024 Flink push made them less Kafka-only and more 'data streaming platform'. The strategic question is whether Kora (the cloud-native broker rewrite) is enough to fend off Redpanda + WarpStream + native S3 brokers.
questions: What does Schema Registry enforce that you can't get from raw Kafka topics?

If lag balloons on one consumer group while throughput is flat, how do you triage?

mongodb.blurb: The document database that became the default OLTP store for a generation of JavaScript-first apps. Replica sets give automatic failover via Raft (post-3.6), sharding spreads load on a chosen key, and WiredTiger underneath handles MVCC + B+tree storage with compression. Atlas (the SaaS) is now the bulk of revenue. The strategic moves of the last few years — multi-document ACID transactions, time-series collections, native vector search — are all aimed at not losing developers to Postgres + JSONB.
questions: Why did MongoDB pick a document model in 2007, and what did the relational orthodoxy underestimate?
How does the replica set election protocol differ from Raft (and why did MongoDB have to invent PV1 first)?
Sharding key choice is the most consequential decision — what happens with a low-cardinality key?
How does WiredTiger's MVCC compare with Postgres MVCC at the page-format level?
What did multi-document ACID transactions cost in latency, and why are they discouraged for hot paths?
Why is the aggregation pipeline more powerful than SQL group-by for nested/array shapes?
Index intersection in MongoDB — when does the optimizer use it, and why isn't it always faster?
How does change streams differ from a tailable cursor on the oplog?
Time-series collections compress how — bucketing or columnar?

Atlas Search is Lucene under the hood — how does it integrate with the document model?
How does MongoDB's vector search compare with Pinecone for embedding workloads?

How does MongoDB handle hot-collection scans without the kind of statistics Postgres collects?

Schema validation is opt-in — what does that imply for production hygiene at scale?

When is Postgres + JSONB the better pick over MongoDB?

elasticsearch.blurb: Distributed Lucene with a JSON API on top — the de facto choice for full-text search and the bottom of the ELK stack for logs. Inverted index + BM25 by default, with a vector engine bolted on (HNSW + dense_vector) for hybrid search. The 2021 SSPL relicense forked OpenSearch out at AWS; the 2024 AGPLv3 move was an attempt to relax that. The cost story is real — log workloads stay there mostly because nothing fully replaces operator familiarity, even as ClickHouse and DuckDB chip away.
questions: What does Lucene's segment-based index do that a traditional B+tree can't for text search?
Why is BM25 still the default ranker after 30 years, and what does dense retrieval actually buy you?
How does a refresh interval interact with the search-vs-index latency trade-off?
What goes wrong when your shard count is too high vs too low?
How does Elasticsearch's HNSW implementation handle filter-then-vector vs vector-then-filter?

What is the actual difference between a query and a filter in the context of caching?

How does cross-cluster search differ semantically from cross-cluster replication?
ILM (Index Lifecycle Management) compresses cold data — how does that play with object storage?
Why is ELK so much more expensive than ClickHouse for the same log volume?
ESQL (the new query language) — what is it actually compiling to?
How does Elasticsearch's vector search compare to a dedicated vector DB at 100M+ embeddings?
When is a tombstone (deleted doc) actually purged, and how does that affect heap?
Why is the JVM heap the hard scaling ceiling, and what's the fix line?
How is the Elastic Agent different from Beats + Logstash architecturally?
Cross-region replication: synchronous vs leader-follower — when is each safe?
What does Elastic's Stack Monitoring observe that Prometheus can't?
If query latency p99 doubles overnight with no ingest change, where do you start?

openai.blurb: The lab that turned 'scale plus next-token prediction' into a product category. From GPT-2 (2019) demonstrating zero-shot transfer, to InstructGPT (2022) productizing RLHF, to GPT-4 (2023) crossing the multimodal + reasoning threshold, OpenAI shipped each step before consensus. The o-series (o1, o3, o4) bet on inference-time chain-of-thought scaling. The Microsoft tie-up gives Azure-exclusive serving for the API; ChatGPT (8B+ MAU by 2025) is what made conversational AI a mass-market UX expectation.
questions: What did GPT-2 prove that the BERT/T5 line missed about generative pretraining?
How does InstructGPT's RLHF pipeline differ from supervised fine-tuning at the loss level?
Why is Kaplan's scaling law incomplete without Chinchilla's compute-optimal correction?
What is o1 actually doing at inference — RL-trained CoT, MCTS, or something else?
How does the Responses API's tool-use loop differ from Chat Completions function calling?
Structured Outputs (JSON schema) — what's the constrained-decoding mechanism?
Why is OpenAI's pricing per-token model so resistant to undercutting?
What did the GPT-4 'system card' deliberately not reveal, and why?
How does the Realtime API solve the duplex-audio problem at <300ms latency?

anthropic.blurb: Founded in 2021 by Dario and Daniela Amodei (ex-OpenAI VPs of Research) plus the GPT-3 paper authors. Identity is built on three legs: (1) Claude as the frontier model line — Sonnet/Opus/Haiku — competitive at the top end, (2) Constitutional AI / RLAIF as an alternative alignment recipe to RLHF, (3) mechanistic interpretability research that opened the inside of transformer circuits in public. Recent platform pushes include MCP (now an industry standard for tool integration), Computer Use, and Claude Code.
questions: What does Constitutional AI replace in the RLHF pipeline, and what does it preserve?
Why is RLAIF cheaper than RLHF in practice, and where does it underperform?

huggingface.blurb: GitHub-for-models. The transformers library standardized loading any pretrained checkpoint with three lines of Python; the Hub became the default place to publish weights, datasets, and Spaces (Gradio demos). Datasets, Tokenizers, Accelerate, PEFT, and TRL fill out the open-source training stack. Strategically: HF survives because it's neutral infrastructure for an open-weights ecosystem that nobody wants to consolidate under a single closed provider — but it monetizes mostly via Inference Endpoints + Enterprise tier.
questions: What did the transformers library standardize that nobody else did, and why was that decisive?

langchain.blurb: Harrison Chase's framework for chaining LLM calls — started as a Python utility belt, now expanded into LangGraph (stateful agent runtime), LangSmith (eval + tracing) and LangChain Cloud (deployment). Heavily criticized for early API churn and over-abstraction; the LangGraph rewrite (2024) is the cleaner successor — explicit graphs, reducer-based state, checkpointing for human-in-the-loop. The product story is: open-source pulls users in, LangSmith monetizes via observability, LangGraph Cloud monetizes via stateful runtime.
questions: Why did LangChain's early `Chain` abstraction get so much heat — what was wrong with the model?

pinecone.blurb: Edo Liberty's bet that vector search deserved its own managed service, before LLMs made it mainstream. The original architecture used HNSW + IVF-style ANN; the 2023 'serverless' rewrite separated vector storage from compute and switched to a proprietary slab format that enables on-disk indexes plus 100× lower idle cost. Hybrid search (sparse + dense) lands BM25 alongside cosine. The competitive squeeze is real — pgvector, Weaviate, Qdrant, Milvus, plus warehouse-native (BigQuery vector, Snowflake Cortex) — but Pinecone holds the 'serverless, no infra' niche.
questions: What does HNSW solve that IVF can't, and where does IVF still win?

cloudflare.blurb: Started as anycast CDN + DDoS shield, expanded into a global edge platform with 300+ PoPs. Workers (V8 isolates, not containers) ship JS/Wasm with cold-start in single-digit ms; R2 is S3 without egress; D1 is SQLite-on-edge; Durable Objects give you single-instance state per name. Pingora replaced the nginx-based proxy in production. Cloudflare's bet is that the edge is the right place to put compute for everything below 'AI training' — and they're now adding Workers AI to chase that ceiling too.
questions: Why are V8 isolates the right cold-start primitive vs containers/Firecracker?
What does Cloudflare's anycast routing buy that DNS-based geo-routing doesn't?

vercel.blurb: Guillermo Rauch's commercialization of Next.js — git push gives you a global preview URL, with Edge Functions, Image Optimization, ISR (incremental static regen), and a CDN sitting in front. Underneath: AWS Lambda for SSR, Cloudflare-style anycast, and a tightly opinionated build pipeline. The 'frontend cloud' positioning lets them charge a premium over raw cloud while owning the framework that drives demand. v0 (AI-generated UI) and the Next.js + Server Components push extend that into the LLM era.

hnsw.blurb: Malkov & Yashunin 2016: a multi-layer proximity graph where each layer is a sparser zoom-out of the next, giving log-N greedy search with surprisingly tight recall. Eats memory (every vector keeps O(M) edges in RAM) but pays back with sub-millisecond latency at million-to-billion scale. The default index in FAISS, Lucene, pgvector, Weaviate, Qdrant, Milvus — basically every vector store in 2026 ships HNSW first and adds IVF/PQ as a memory-budget escape hatch.

ivf-pq.blurb: The classic FAISS recipe: IVF partitions the space into nlist Voronoi cells via k-means so search only scans nprobe of them, and PQ (Jégou 2011) compresses each residual into M sub-quantizers' codebook indices for ~16-32× memory shrink. Less popular than HNSW for low-latency QPS, but still the right answer for billion-scale on a single box, GPU search, and any workload where vector storage cost dominates query latency.
questions: What's the partition idea behind IVF, and why does increasing nprobe trade recall for latency almost linearly?

faiss.blurb: Johnson, Douze & Jégou 2017 at Facebook: the C++/CUDA library that turned billion-scale ANN from a research curiosity into a one-liner. Implements IVF, PQ, OPQ, HNSW, ScaNN-style, plus the GPU IVFPQ kernels nothing else has matched. It's not a database — no persistence, no concurrency, no filtering — but it's the substrate every vector DB on this list either wraps, ports, or benchmarks against. Still the right answer in 2026 for offline batch retrieval, eval pipelines, and anyone who needs the lowest layer.

milvus.blurb: Zilliz's open-source vector DB, originally a 2019 fork of Knowhere (their FAISS-derived engine) and rewritten 2.x as a cloud-native distributed system: stateless query/data nodes, pluggable object storage (S3/MinIO), Pulsar/Kafka for log replay, etcd for metadata. Supports HNSW, IVF, DiskANN, GPU indexes, and SCANN out of the box. The most operationally serious open-source vector DB in 2026 — the bet is that vector workloads need a separated-compute architecture like Snowflake, not a single binary like Postgres.

weaviate.blurb: Bob van Luijt's open-source vector DB, Go-native, schema-first, with built-in modules for embedding generation (text2vec-openai, ref2vec, multi2vec-clip) and a hybrid BM25+dense search baked into the query API rather than bolted on. The bet was always 'vectors are a property of objects, not the object itself' — so Weaviate looks more like an object DB with vectors than FAISS-as-a-service. Strong on hybrid retrieval and GraphQL ergonomics; weaker on raw QPS than Qdrant.

What's the right backup/restore story for a multi-class Weaviate cluster — schema + vectors or just snapshots?
How does Weaviate handle concept drift when the embedding module updates a model version?
If hybrid recall regresses after enabling a new module, where do you look — alpha, BM25 stats, or schema indexing flags?

qdrant.blurb: Andrey Vasnetsov's Rust-native vector DB, bet on payload (metadata) as a first-class index alongside vectors. Filtering is genuinely fast because the JSON payload index is co-located with HNSW and the engine pushes the predicate into the graph traversal (filterable HNSW), not after. Strong on QPS, predictable tail latency, single-binary deploy, and a UX that respects engineers — fewer modules than Weaviate, more thought put into filter performance than Pinecone. The default 'I want it to feel like Postgres but for vectors' choice in 2026.
questions: Why is Qdrant's filtered search faster than naive post-filter HNSW under low-selectivity predicates?
Walk through how the payload index integrates with the HNSW graph traversal — where does the predicate get evaluated?
How does Qdrant's scalar / binary / product quantization composition compare with FAISS's IVFPQ?
Why did Qdrant pick Rust + Tokio, and what concretely went better than a Go or C++ choice?
What does a Qdrant 'collection' map to operationally — shard count, replication factor, payload schema?
How does Qdrant handle multi-tenancy — payload-based filtering vs collection-per-tenant, and the cost of each?
What's the operational reason Qdrant scales better than Weaviate under sustained write + filter workload?

Why does binary quantization on 1024-dim vectors give 32× compression with surprisingly small recall hit?
How does Qdrant's distributed mode shard — by point_id hash, by payload, or by vector partition?
When does Qdrant's filterable HNSW degenerate to brute force, and what selectivity threshold triggers that?
How would you migrate from Pinecone serverless to self-hosted Qdrant — what's the data and API parity gap?

When recall regresses after enabling quantization, where do you look — rescoring config, oversampling, or quantizer training?
Is Qdrant's payload-as-first-class bet defensible against pgvector adding HNSW filter pushdown?

chroma.blurb: Jeff Huber & Anton Troynikov's Python-first embedding DB, originally an in-process SQLite-backed store optimized for the local-RAG developer experience. The 2024 distributed rewrite (Rust core, log-structured architecture, S3-backed) tried to scale up without losing the 'pip install chromadb' onboarding. Still the right choice for prototypes and single-tenant apps; arguably oversold for serious production, where the lack of mature filtering and ops tooling shows.
questions: What's Chroma's actual storage layer — SQLite, DuckDB, or the new distributed Rust core, and when does each kick in?
Why did Chroma start with in-process Python instead of a server-first architecture like Qdrant or Weaviate?
Walk through Chroma's embedding function abstraction — what does it solve and what does it lock you into?

pgvector.blurb: Andrew Kane's Postgres extension: vector type, ivfflat (since 0.4) and HNSW (since 0.5.0, 2023) indexes, pg_vector_cosine and friends. The 'boring' choice that turned out to be correct for most teams — operational maturity, transactions, joins with relational data, no second system to back up. Scaling ceiling sits around tens of millions of vectors per node before HNSW build cost and bloat become painful; above that, dedicated vector DBs win. In 2026 the default starting point for any RAG system that already has Postgres.
questions: Why is pgvector the right default — what does 'just another Postgres index' buy you operationally?
How does pgvector's HNSW implementation compare to FAISS's — what's missing or different?
When does ivfflat beat HNSW in pgvector, and when is it the wrong choice?

sentence-transformers.blurb: Reimers & Gurevych 2019: a SBERT framework that fine-tunes BERT-style encoders with siamese / triplet objectives to produce sentence embeddings whose cosine similarity is meaningful — solving BERT's [CLS] mediocrity for retrieval. The library became the default workhorse for text encoders, and its descendants (BGE, E5, GTE, mxbai-embed) still anchor most production RAG pipelines in 2026, even as OpenAI's text-embedding-3 and Cohere embed-v3 dominate the closed-source side.

Bi-encoder vs cross-encoder — when do you use each, and what's the latency/quality trade?
Why is mean pooling usually better than [CLS] for sentence embeddings, and where does CLS still win?

colbert.blurb: Khattab & Zaharia 2020: late interaction retrieval. Encode each token (not the whole sentence) into a vector, then score query-document pairs via MaxSim — the sum of each query token's max similarity to any document token. Recovers most of the cross-encoder's quality at bi-encoder cost, at the price of storing N_tokens × dim per document. ColBERTv2 (2022) added centroid-based PQ compression that made it viable at scale; PLAID (2022) made it actually fast. Still niche in 2026 but the right answer when retrieval quality matters more than vector storage cost.
questions: Why does late interaction beat bi-encoder retrieval on hard queries — what's the geometric reason MaxSim works?

rag.blurb: Lewis et al 2020: retrieve relevant documents, condition an LLM on them, generate. The 'just look it up' paradigm that papered over LLM hallucination and made enterprise GenAI commercially viable. The 2020 paper used a fixed retriever; the 2023-2026 wave (Self-RAG, GraphRAG, Agentic RAG, hybrid + rerank stacks) made retrieval iterative, multimodal, and structured. Still the dominant architecture for enterprise LLM apps in 2026 even as long-context models try to make it obsolete and largely fail at the cost/latency curve.
questions: What did the original Lewis 2020 RAG actually train end-to-end vs the modular RAG of 2026?
Why is chunking the most overlooked and most important parameter in production RAG — what's the right unit?
Walk through hybrid retrieval (BM25 + dense) — when does each lane catch what the other misses?

llamaindex.blurb: Jerry Liu's framework, originally GPT Index in 2022, that became the opinionated alternative to LangChain for RAG-first applications. Tighter primitives around documents, nodes, indexes, and query engines; more thoughtful abstractions for multi-document agents, structured retrieval, and graph-based RAG. By 2026 it's matured into a serious enterprise platform (LlamaCloud, LlamaParse, agent toolkits) competing with LangGraph, Haystack, and the in-house frameworks every company eventually writes.
questions: What does LlamaIndex's Document → Node → Index abstraction get right that LangChain's flat retriever doesn't?
When is a Sub-Question Query Engine actually better than just stuffing more context into a single retrieve-then-generate call?
How does LlamaParse handle complex PDFs (tables, multi-column, figures) compared to PyPDF or Unstructured?
Walk through a multi-document router: how does the router pick which index to query, and what's the failure mode?

paged-attention.blurb: Kwon et al 2023 (vLLM): treat the KV cache like OS virtual memory — fixed-size physical blocks (typically 16 tokens) plus a per-sequence block table mapping logical token positions to physical pages. Collapses external fragmentation from 60-80% of GPU memory waste down to ~4%, which is what made high-batch LLM serving viable. Now table-stakes — every serious inference engine (TRT-LLM, SGLang, TGI, MLC) ships its own variant.

Why is the default block size 16 tokens — what's the trade-off between internal fragmentation and block-table indirection cost?
How does PagedAttention enable copy-on-write KV sharing for parallel sampling (n>1, beam search)?

prefix-caching.blurb: Reuse the KV cache across requests that share a prefix — system prompt, few-shot examples, RAG context. SGLang's RadixAttention (2024) made the radix-tree lookup the first-class scheduler primitive; vLLM V1 promoted automatic prefix caching to default-on with hash-keyed block sharing. On agent / chat workloads with long fixed prefixes, hit rates routinely exceed 80% and TTFT drops 5-10×. The win disappears when prefixes jitter (timestamp in system prompt) — measure hit rate, don't assume it.
questions: Why is prefix caching purely a prefill-side optimization — what does it not help in decode?
Walk through RadixAttention: how does the radix tree map shared token sequences to shared KV blocks?
How does vLLM's hash-keyed block sharing differ structurally from SGLang's radix tree?

bpe-tokenization.blurb: Sennrich et al 2016 brought BPE from compression into NMT; it's still the dominant subword scheme. Variants: byte-level BPE (GPT-2), SentencePiece unigram (T5, Llama, Gemma), tiktoken (OpenAI's optimized BPE in Rust). The tokenizer is the silent boundary between text and the model — it shapes vocab cost, multilingual fairness, code performance, and the dreaded 'glitch token' failures (SolidGoldMagikarp). Llama 3's 128k vocab is itself a capability lever.
questions: Walk through BPE training: how do merge rules get learned greedily from a corpus?

gptq.blurb: Frantar et al 2022: post-training INT4 weight quantization that solves a layer-wise reconstruction problem with a Hessian-aware OBS-style update — quantize one column at a time, then update remaining columns to compensate. Gets a 175B OPT down to ~3.5 bits/weight with minimal perplexity loss in hours, no fine-tuning. Still the workhorse for W4A16 serving in 2026 alongside AWQ; both ship as standard backends in vLLM, TRT-LLM, and llama.cpp.

Walk through the OBS-style column update — why does compensating remaining columns recover most of the lost accuracy?
Why is the inverse-Hessian computation tractable here — what structure of W X^T X are we exploiting?

awq.blurb: Lin et al 2023: activation-aware weight quantization. Observation: a small fraction (~1%) of weight channels are 'salient' — those whose corresponding activation magnitudes are large — and quantizing them naively destroys quality. AWQ scales those channels up before quantization (and inversely scales activations) to protect them. No backward pass, no Hessian; faster than GPTQ to produce, often slightly better quality, and the default INT4 path in many 2026 deployments alongside GPTQ.
questions: What's the core insight — why is 'salience' defined by activation magnitude, not weight magnitude?
Walk through the per-channel scaling: how does scaling W up and X down preserve the matmul exactly?
How is the optimal scaling factor s* chosen — grid search on calibration loss?
Why does AWQ skip the Hessian — what structural assumption replaces GPTQ's reconstruction objective?
Compare AWQ to SmoothQuant — both rescale activation outliers, what's actually different?
Why does AWQ often outperform GPTQ on instruction-tuned models — is it the activation distribution shift?
What changes when applying AWQ to attention vs FFN — does Q/K/V need different treatment?

smoothquant.blurb: Xiao et al 2022: solve the activation-outlier problem that breaks naive INT8 LLM quantization. Activations have channel-wise outliers ~100× larger than the median; weights are tame. SmoothQuant migrates the outlier difficulty from activations to weights via a per-channel scale s, exploiting the mathematical equivalence (X·diag(s)) · (diag(1/s)·W) = X·W. Result: stable W8A8 INT8 inference at near-fp16 quality, the precursor to AWQ's salience-aware framing.
questions: Why do LLM activations have ~100× channel outliers while weights don't — what causes this asymmetry?
Walk through the equivalence (X·S) · (S^-1·W) — why is it exact, not approximate?
How is the scaling factor α chosen (typically 0.5) — what does it trade off?

What does the open-source ecosystem support look like — vLLM, TRT-LLM, llama.cpp paths?

gguf.blurb: GGUF (GPT-Generated Unified Format) replaced GGML in 2023 as the on-disk format for llama.cpp and its ecosystem (Ollama, LM Studio, koboldcpp). Single-file, mmap-friendly, self-describing metadata (architecture, tokenizer, chat template), tensor-level versioned quantization types (Q4_K_M, Q5_K_S, IQ2_XXS, ...). The de facto interchange format for local / consumer-GPU LLM serving — if you download a model from TheBloke / bartowski, it's GGUF.
questions: Why did GGML get replaced by GGUF — what was structurally wrong with the old format?

Open question: does GGUF survive native FP4 hardware, or does the format need a v2?

llama-cpp.blurb: Georgi Gerganov's pure-C++ LLM runtime: started March 2023 as 'can we run Llama on a MacBook?' and became the dominant local inference engine. CPU SIMD (AVX-512, NEON), Apple Metal, CUDA, ROCm, Vulkan, SYCL all share one codebase. Powers Ollama, LM Studio, llamafile, jan.ai. The backbone of the consumer / edge / desktop LLM ecosystem in 2026, while vLLM owns the datacenter side.
questions: Why did llama.cpp pick C++ with zero deps over Python + PyTorch — what does that buy and cost?
Walk through the ggml graph: how are ops registered, scheduled, and dispatched to backends?
How does the CPU SIMD path achieve competitive throughput on Apple Silicon vs Metal?

ollama.blurb: Jeffrey Morgan's CLI/server that wraps llama.cpp into a Docker-for-models UX: `ollama pull llama3`, `ollama run`, OpenAI-compatible HTTP API, Modelfile templates for system prompt + params. The default on-ramp for local LLM dev in 2026 — every dev tool that 'supports local models' means it speaks Ollama's API. Trade-off: easy at the cost of llama.cpp's full knob surface, and the registry's quant choices aren't always the right ones.

sglang.blurb: Lianmin Zheng et al (UC Berkeley) 2024: an inference engine that treats RadixAttention (prefix-cache-as-radix-tree) as the scheduler primitive, not an afterthought. Frontend DSL for structured / multi-turn / agentic flows compiles to KV-aware execution plans. Strong on long-prefix workloads (RAG, agents) where its hit rates beat vLLM's hash-based scheme; structured generation is first-class via constrained decoding. The third pillar of self-hosted serving in 2026 next to vLLM and TRT-LLM.

tgi.blurb: HuggingFace Text Generation Inference: Rust router + Python (formerly with custom CUDA) inference workers, designed for production serving on the HF Hub. v3 (2024) rebuilt around continuous batching with PagedAttention, prefix caching, FlashDecoding, plus first-class support for HF tokenizers and quantization formats (GPTQ, AWQ, EETQ, FP8). Trails vLLM / SGLang on raw throughput in 2026 but owns the integration story for HF Hub deployments and the HF Inference Endpoints product.

mlc-llm.blurb: TVM-based LLM compiler from CMU's Apache TVM team: compile a model once, deploy across WebGPU (browser), iOS / Android, CUDA, ROCm, Metal, Vulkan from a single source. Auto-scheduling and tensor IR-level fusion let it land competitive perf on platforms where vLLM doesn't run at all. The pragmatic answer when 'I need this 7B model in a browser tab' or 'on a Pixel phone' actually shows up — not the answer for datacenter throughput.

prefill-decode-disagg.blurb: Splitwise (MSR 2023) and DistServe (2024) noticed that prefill is compute-bound and decode is memory-bandwidth-bound, so co-locating them on one GPU pool wastes both. Disaggregated serving runs separate prefill and decode worker pools, transferring KV cache over NVLink / IB / RDMA after prefill. Mooncake (Moonshot 2024) productized this for Kimi at scale. In 2026 it's the frontier pattern for long-context, high-traffic LLM serving — vLLM and SGLang both ship disaggregated modes.

tensorflow.blurb: Google Brain 2015: static-graph DAG with Sessions, plus Keras as the high-level API and TF Serving / TFLite / TF.js as the deployment fan-out. TF 2 conceded to imperative execution after PyTorch took the research mindshare; by 2026 it survives mostly inside Google product surfaces, mobile (TFLite / LiteRT on Android / Pixel), and the TPU stack via XLA. Outside that triangle nearly every new project starts in PyTorch or JAX.

keras.blurb: Chollet 2015: a high-level API that hid backends (Theano, then TF) behind Sequential / Functional / Model. Keras 3 (2023) re-pluralized backends — TF, JAX, PyTorch — making it the only mainstream API where the same code trains on TPU via JAX and ships to mobile via TFLite. It owns intro-DL pedagogy and Kaggle starter notebooks; serious frontier work still drops to native PyTorch / JAX once kernel-level control matters.

lightning.blurb: William Falcon's 2019 trainer wrapper around PyTorch: LightningModule splits research code (forward, train/val/test step) from engineering boilerplate (loop, distributed, mixed precision, checkpointing). The Trainer handles DDP / FSDP / DeepSpeed plug-in, gradient accumulation, AMP, and callbacks. Still the default for medium-scale academic / industry training; Hugging Face Accelerate competes on the lightweight end and torchtitan / native FSDP2 takes the frontier-scale end.

accelerate.blurb: Hugging Face's lightweight launcher: a few-line wrapper over torch.distributed that hides DDP / FSDP / DeepSpeed / Megatron-LM choice behind accelerate config. No Trainer, no LightningModule — you keep your training loop and call accelerator.prepare() on model/optimizer/dataloader. The default substrate for HF Trainer, TRL (DPO/PPO/GRPO), diffusers, and most 7B-70B fine-tuning recipes since 2023.

colossal-ai.blurb: HPC-AI Tech's open-source large-model stack: combines TP / PP / SP, ZeRO, gradient checkpointing, and CPU/NVMe offload behind a unified config. Targets the gap between DeepSpeed (single-engine) and Megatron-LM (NVIDIA-only) for users without FAANG-scale clusters. Notable for early Stable Diffusion / ChatGPT-style replication recipes; in 2026 it's a niche pick — most teams have settled on FSDP2 + Megatron-Core or vLLM-side optimizations.

wandb.blurb: Lukas Biewer's 2018 experiment-tracking SaaS: log scalars, gradients, system metrics, artifacts, and rich media (images / audio / 3D / tables) into versioned runs with automatic config / git / env capture. Sweeps cover hyperparameter search; Reports / Workspaces handle collaboration. The default tracker for HF Trainer, Lightning, Accelerate, TRL — competes with MLflow on OSS / on-prem and Comet / Neptune on UX. Acquired by CoreWeave in 2024.

mlflow.blurb: Databricks 2018: open-source MLOps with four loosely-coupled components — Tracking (runs / params / metrics), Model Registry (versions / stages / aliases), Projects, and Models (the flavor abstraction). The default on-prem / open-source counterpart to W&B, glue-code-friendly via REST + Python SDK, and the de facto registry inside Databricks. Recent versions added LLM eval, prompt versioning, and tracing for agents — chasing the LLMOps niche LangSmith and W&B Weave already occupy.

mixed-precision.blurb: Train weights in FP32 while running activations / gradients in FP16 / BF16 / FP8 to halve memory and double throughput on Tensor Cores. FP16 needs loss scaling to avoid gradient underflow; BF16 has FP32's exponent range so scaling is unnecessary — that's why every modern LLM pretrain uses BF16. FP8 (E4M3 forward, E5M2 backward) is the H100/B200 default via NVIDIA Transformer Engine; per-tensor / per-block scaling is now the bottleneck, not the math.

tensor-parallel.blurb: Megatron-style intra-layer split: column-parallel slices the QKV / FFN-up GEMM along output channels, row-parallel slices the projection / FFN-down along input channels — so each layer needs exactly one all-reduce in forward and one in backward. TP scales linearly with NVLink bandwidth, which caps it at ~8 GPUs per node before all-reduce becomes the bottleneck. Stacked with sequence-parallel to recover LayerNorm/Dropout activation memory, it's the inner layer of every modern 3D parallelism plan.

pipeline-parallel.blurb: Split layers across stages and stream micro-batches through them: forward then backward in a 1F1B schedule (Megatron) or interleaved (virtual pipeline stages) to shrink the bubble. PP trades activation memory for cross-node bandwidth — unlike TP it scales beyond a single NVLink island, so it's the parallelism dimension that crosses node boundaries in 3D parallelism. Bubble = (P-1)/(M+P-1); micro-batch count M is the only knob without re-architecting the model.

fsdp.blurb: PyTorch's native ZeRO-3: shard parameters / gradients / optimizer state across DP ranks with all-gather-before-forward and reduce-scatter-after-backward. FSDP2 (per-parameter sharding via DTensor) replaced FSDP1's flat-param hack in 2024, fixing checkpoint format, mixed-dtype, and TP composition. The default training stack inside HF Accelerate, Lightning, torchtitan, and Llama-3 reference code — DeepSpeed ZeRO-3 has been largely displaced for new projects.

gradient-checkpointing.blurb: Trade compute for memory: don't save activations during forward, recompute them during backward. The memory drop is from O(L) to O(sqrt(L)) under Chen et al's 2016 selective scheme; the compute cost is one extra forward pass (~33% wall-clock). Selective recompute (recompute attention only, save the rest) is the modern Megatron / FSDP default — full recompute is rarely needed once you have FSDP + sequence parallel.

adamw.blurb: Loshchilov & Hutter 2017 (ICLR 2019): Adam's L2 regularization is mathematically not weight decay because the second-moment denominator scales it; AdamW decouples decay from gradient and applies it directly to the weights. The de facto optimizer for every frontier LLM since GPT-3 — Llama, GPT-4, Gemini, Claude all train with AdamW. Optimizer state (m, v, fp32 master) is 8 bytes/param, the dominant memory cost ZeRO and 8-bit-Adam exist to amortize.

lr-schedule.blurb: Warmup → main shape → decay. Linear warmup over 0.1-1% of steps prevents early-divergence (Adam's second moment is unwarmed); cosine decay to ~10% of peak became the GPT-3-era default; WSD (Warmup-Stable-Decay, MiniCPM 2024) flattens the middle to enable continual training without re-warmup. Schedule choice is load-bearing — same model + same data + wrong schedule = 0.5 bpb worse loss.

rope.blurb: Su et al. 2021: encode position by rotating each (q, k) pair by an angle proportional to position * frequency, so the dot product q·k depends only on relative offset. Used by Llama, GPT-NeoX, Qwen, DeepSeek, Mistral — every modern open LLM. Long-context extension (16k → 1M) reuses RoPE via base-frequency scaling: NTK-aware (Bloc97 2023), YaRN (Peng 2023), Llama-3.1's extension to 128k. The default position encoding for autoregressive LLMs in 2026.

rmsnorm.blurb: Zhang & Sennrich 2019: drop LayerNorm's mean-centering and bias, just scale by RMS(x) = sqrt(mean(x²)). 7-64% faster than LayerNorm with no quality loss; that's why Llama, Mistral, Gemma, Qwen, DeepSeek all use it. Pre-norm placement (norm before attention/FFN, residual outside) is now universal — post-norm trains worse beyond ~24 layers. The 2019 paper sat unused until Llama 1 made it the open-LLM default in 2023.

nvidia-gpu.blurb: The dominant ML accelerator: SM (Streaming Multiprocessor) with warp-level SIMT, register file, shared memory as scratchpad, and Tensor Cores as the matmul ASIC. Hopper H100 (2022) added FP8, TMA, and async warp specialization; Blackwell B200 (2024) doubled memory bandwidth, added FP4, and introduced 2-CTA cluster MMA. CUDA + cuDNN + cuBLAS + NCCL is the stack the entire ML ecosystem boots on — and Nvidia's moat in 2026.

tpu.blurb: Google's in-house accelerator: systolic-array MXU (matrix multiply unit) + HBM + ICI (Inter-Chip Interconnect for 3D-torus pod topology). v5p / v6 (Trillium) push individual chip BF16 FLOPs into the H100 range and pod size to 8960 chips (v5p) with full ICI mesh. The platform behind every Google frontier model — Gemini, AlphaFold, Pathways — and the only mainstream alternative to NVIDIA at training scale; everything goes through XLA / JAX.

gpu-interconnect.blurb: The fabric every distributed training run actually rides: NVLink (intra-node, 900GB/s on H100, 1.8TB/s on B200) + NVSwitch fabric for full-mesh within a node, InfiniBand HDR/NDR (200-400 Gb/s) + GPUDirect RDMA across nodes, and SHARP for in-network reduction at 1k+ GPU scale. NCCL picks ring vs tree vs NVLS based on this topology. The bottleneck that decides whether 1024 GPUs train at 0.5x or 0.95x of perfect linear scaling.

attention.blurb: Bahdanau, Cho & Bengio 2014: a learned alignment between encoder hidden states and a decoding step, originally bolted onto a seq2seq RNN to fix the fixed-length-bottleneck problem in neural machine translation. The bare mechanism — softmax-weighted average over a value sequence, conditioned on a query — predates the Transformer by three years and is the conceptual seed of every modern attention-based architecture, including FlashAttention's tiled implementation of the same arithmetic.
questions: What problem in seq2seq did Bahdanau attention actually fix — and why was the fixed-length context vector the bottleneck?

What's the difference between soft, hard, and local attention, and why do gradients break for hard attention?
How does the alignment matrix in attention reveal interpretability that pure RNN states don't?
Why was attention an 'auxiliary' module in 2014 NMT but a load-bearing primitive by 2017?
Derive the gradient of softmax(QK^T)V w.r.t. Q — where does the Jacobian get expensive?
Why does additive attention scale as O(NM·d) while dot-product is O(NM·d) but matmul-friendly — what's the hardware difference?
How does cross-attention between encoder and decoder differ from self-attention inside one stack?
Why does temperature/scaling on the alignment score matter, and what does saturation look like?
Sparse attention (top-k, locality, blockwise) — when does it preserve and when does it break the soft-attention semantics?
How is attention's ability to handle variable-length input fundamentally different from a fixed-size GRU encoding?
What is the connection between attention and kernel smoothing / Nadaraya-Watson estimation?
Why does stacking attention layers (vs a single attention head) buy compositional reasoning, not just more capacity?
How would you visualize attention weights in production to debug a translation model that drops named entities?
Where does Bahdanau-style attention still survive in 2026 — speech recognition, pointer networks, GraphNet message passing?
Why is the additive form sometimes preferred for tiny models (low d) despite dot-product's hardware advantage?
How does attention relate to the older idea of memory networks / NTMs — what did each give up?
Is attention a content-based addressing scheme, a soft kNN, or a learned routing mechanism — and does the answer change anything practical?

self-attention.blurb: The Transformer-style variant where Q, K, and V are all linear projections of the same input sequence, letting every position aggregate information from every other position in a single layer. Vaswani et al 2017 framed it as 'attention is all you need' precisely because self-attention removes the recurrence bottleneck and makes the dependency graph a complete bipartite. Every subsequent architectural debate — sparse attention, linear attention, Mamba, RWKV — is framed as 'what self-attention costs you and how to pay less'.
questions: Why is self-attention a token mixer while FFN is a channel mixer — and why does that decomposition matter?
What does causal masking change about self-attention's expressivity, and why must inference for decoder LLMs be autoregressive?
Walk through self-attention's O(N²) compute and O(N²) memory — which one actually bottlenecks training vs inference?
Why does multi-head split d into h subspaces, and what does each head empirically learn (induction, copy, position)?
How does FlashAttention preserve exact self-attention semantics while never materializing the N×N matrix?
Why does softmax inside self-attention act as a soft-nearest-neighbour over keys, and what's the temperature interpretation?
Can you derive self-attention as a special case of a graph neural net with a learned, dense adjacency?
What does the rank of the attention output collapse to as depth grows, and why does this motivate residual + LayerNorm?
Why does cross-attention generalize to encoder-decoder while self-attention generalizes to decoder-only LLMs?
How do GQA / MQA fold the K and V projections to reduce KV-cache memory at the cost of expressiveness?
Is self-attention permutation-equivariant, and what does that say about the necessity of positional encoding?
Why is the softmax-induced quadratic cost not the only quadratic — what about the activation memory of QK^T?
What does linear attention (Performer, Linear Transformer) trade away from full softmax self-attention, and where does it bite?
How does attention sink (Xiao 2024) emerge from self-attention's softmax constraint that scores must sum to 1?
When debugging a model with collapsed attention entropy, what training-time fixes (warmup, init, clipping) actually help?
Why does self-attention love matmul-heavy hardware (Tensor Cores) while RNNs do not — derive from arithmetic intensity?
How would you redesign self-attention if you had to support 1M tokens of context on a single H100?
Is self-attention fundamental, or is it the local optimum the field is stuck on because of GPU shape preferences?

positional-encoding.blurb: Self-attention is permutation-equivariant; positional encoding is the patch that injects order. Vaswani 2017 used sinusoidal absolute encodings; learned absolute embeddings (BERT, GPT-2) followed; RoPE (Su 2021) made relative position multiplicative inside the QK product and dominates open LLMs (Llama, Mistral, Qwen, DeepSeek); ALiBi (Press 2022) does the same with a linear bias. The choice now lives at the center of every long-context story, with YaRN, NTK-aware scaling, and theta tuning being the standard lever for stretching a 4k model to 128k+.
questions: Why is self-attention permutation-equivariant without positional encoding, and what tasks immediately break?
What did sinusoidal PE buy over learned absolute PE — and why is the 'extrapolation' claim from the original paper somewhat overstated?
Walk through RoPE's complex-rotation derivation — why does the inner product depend only on relative position?
Why has RoPE become the de-facto open-LLM default while ALiBi mostly stayed in the BLOOM / MPT lineage?
What does ALiBi's linear bias buy in practice for length extrapolation, and where does it fail compared to RoPE+YaRN?
How does NTK-aware / YaRN base-frequency scaling extend RoPE from 4k to 128k without retraining the whole model?
What's the failure signature when you scale RoPE theta wrong — repetition, drift, attention sink growth?
Why is theta tuning (Llama 3's 500000 vs Llama 2's 10000) effectively a long-context unlock, and what do you give up?
How does relative positional bias (T5) compare to RoPE on long-range retrieval — empirical not just theoretical?
Why is positional encoding frozen for inference but a hyperparameter at training time — and where does that asymmetry leak?
How does positional encoding interact with KV cache reuse and prefix caching across requests?
What changes for vision (ViT 2D positions, axial PE) and audio (relative PE in Whisper) compared to text?
Why is mixing absolute and relative PE (Hybrid PE, DeBERTa-style disentangled) sometimes better than either alone?
How would you debug a long-context model that scores fine on perplexity but fails needle-in-haystack at 64k?
Why is positional encoding the first thing to revisit when extending Llama 3 8B from 8k to 128k context?
What does the position-only attention head literature (Voita) tell us about how PE is actually used inside the model?
Can self-attention learn position implicitly through the causal mask alone, and what does the no-PE GPT result (Haviv 2022) show?
Is PE a fundamental ingredient or a workaround for an architecture that should have been order-aware to begin with?

seq2seq.blurb: Sutskever, Vinyals & Le 2014: stack two LSTMs, encode the source sentence into a fixed-length vector, decode the target token by token. The first competitive neural machine-translation system and the structural template — encoder + decoder + autoregressive sampling — that the Transformer kept and the entire LLM era inherited. The fixed-vector bottleneck it introduced is exactly what Bahdanau attention fixed a few months later, kicking off the chain that ended at 'attention is all you need'.
questions: Why did Sutskever reverse the source sentence — what gradient-flow problem did that solve in the LSTM encoder?
Walk through the fixed-length context bottleneck — why does it kill performance on long sentences specifically?
Why is teacher forcing standard during training but a known cause of exposure bias at inference?
How does beam search trade off length normalization against coverage in NMT — and why does greedy often suffice for chat LLMs?
What did seq2seq + attention (Bahdanau, Luong) preserve from the original architecture and what did it discard?
Why is the encoder-decoder template still preferred for translation / summarization, while decoder-only won general LLMs?
How does seq2seq's autoregressive decoding map onto modern KV cache reuse — what's the structural similarity?
What does scheduled sampling try to fix about teacher forcing, and why hasn't it become standard practice?
How would label smoothing change the seq2seq output distribution, and why is it nearly mandatory for NMT?
Why does seq2seq's vocabulary handling (BPE, SentencePiece) end up mattering more than the architecture choice?
What's the structural difference between seq2seq and a pointer network, and which one fits an extractive QA task?
How does length penalty in beam search interact with subword tokenization in a multilingual model?
When migrating from a seq2seq model to a decoder-only LLM for a translation product, what regressions should you specifically test?
How does seq2seq's training loop differ from a modern instruction-tuned LLM's SFT loop — same loss, different framing?
Why is BLEU a flawed metric for seq2seq output, and what does COMET / chrF++ buy in 2026?
How would you build a seq2seq-style document summarizer on top of a pretrained encoder-decoder (T5, BART) today?
Is encoder-decoder a fundamentally better inductive bias for conditional generation, or did decoder-only just win on infrastructure compatibility?
What's the right way to think about seq2seq in the era of encoder-free 'completion' LLMs — vestigial or lurking under every chat template?

rnn-lstm.blurb: Recurrent networks process sequences with a hidden state carried step by step; LSTM (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber 1997) added input/forget/output gates to solve vanishing gradients, GRU (Cho 2014) simplified to two gates. The dominant sequence model from 2013-2017 (NMT, speech, language modeling) before Transformer wiped them out at scale. Still alive in latency-critical streaming workloads (real-time speech recognizers, low-power edge devices) and as the conceptual ancestor of every state-space model — Mamba's recurrence is mathematically a structured RNN.
questions: Why do vanilla RNN gradients vanish or explode through time — what does the eigenvalue analysis of the recurrent Jacobian tell you?
Walk through LSTM's input/forget/output gates — which one is doing the actual work, and why did GRU drop one?
What is BPTT, and why is truncation necessary in practice — what dependency range does it actually cost you?
Why did LSTMs dominate NMT from 2014-2017 and then disappear almost overnight when Transformer arrived?
How does the hidden-state bottleneck of an RNN map onto the fixed-vector bottleneck that Bahdanau attention fixed?
Why is an RNN's O(N) sequential dependency a death sentence for GPU training compared to Transformer's parallel attention?
What did the LSTM-vs-GRU empirical literature actually conclude — is GRU strictly worse, or task-dependent?
How does QRNN / SRU / IndRNN try to recover RNN's parallelism, and why did none of them stick?
Why is teacher forcing for RNNs essential during training and a known liability at inference?
What is the connection between LSTM's forget gate and the modern selective-scan operator in Mamba?
How would you debug an LSTM that trains fine on length-50 sequences but fails on length-500?
Why are RNNs still the right answer for streaming ASR / on-device keyword spotting in 2026?
Is RWKV a real RNN with attention-level performance, or a Transformer wearing a recurrent disguise?
What does Mamba's linear-recurrence formulation borrow from LSTM, and what is genuinely new (selective Δ)?
How does an RNN's KV-cache equivalent (the hidden state) differ in memory cost from a Transformer's KV cache?
Why does layer normalization placement (pre-cell vs post-cell) matter so much for deep stacked LSTMs?
In a 2026 codebase, when would you pick a small LSTM over a transformer-distilled tiny model?
Did the RNN era leave any architectural lessons (gating, residual recurrence, state compression) that the Transformer era is now relearning?

cnn.blurb: LeCun's 1989 LeNet established the convolutional template — local receptive fields, weight sharing, translation equivariance, hierarchical feature extraction — that AlexNet (2012) scaled into the deep-learning revolution. CNNs ruled vision until ViT (2020) and now sit alongside Transformers as the other workhorse: still the default for low-latency edge inference, U-Net diffusion backbones (until DiT), and dense prediction tasks where the inductive bias still pays. ConvNeXt (2022) showed a 'transformer-tuned' CNN can keep pace at ImageNet scale.
questions: Walk through what weight sharing buys you — parameter count, sample efficiency, and translation equivariance — vs a fully connected layer.
Why is the receptive field the right way to reason about a CNN's effective context, and what does dilation buy?
Why does max-pooling beat average-pooling for classification but lose to strided convs in modern architectures?
How did the 11×11 → 3×3 → patch transition (AlexNet → VGG → ResNet → ViT) play out, and what was the inductive-bias tradeoff?
Why is translation equivariance a useful prior for natural images but actively harmful for OCR / structured documents?
What does depthwise-separable convolution (Xception, MobileNet) decompose, and when does the FLOPs win not translate to wall-clock?
How does the CNN inductive bias compare to ViT's lack of one — and at what data scale does the bias stop helping?
Why is BatchNorm so tightly coupled to CNN pipelines, and what changes when you swap to LayerNorm (ConvNeXt style)?
What did dilated / atrous convolutions buy for semantic segmentation that pooling couldn't?
How does a CNN's effective receptive field grow sub-linearly with depth, and why does that motivate skip connections?
Why did Group Convolution (ResNeXt, MobileNet) become a standard tool in efficient architectures?
How does CNN inference latency compare to Transformer at the same accuracy on edge silicon — what's the bandwidth story?
Why does the U-Net's encoder-decoder + skip pattern dominate dense prediction (segmentation, diffusion) even after ViT?
What does ConvNeXt show about whether the 'CNN vs Transformer' gap was architectural or recipe-driven?
How would you quantize a CNN inference graph — what makes BN folding subtle and where does INT8 actually break?
Why is data augmentation (Mixup, CutMix, RandAugment) a much bigger lever for CNNs than the conv-cell choice?
When deploying a vision model on a $1 microcontroller, why is a small CNN almost always still the right answer?
Is the convolutional inductive bias fundamental for vision, or just convenient — would a 2026 redesign reach for it again?

u-net.blurb: Ronneberger, Fischer & Brox 2015: a symmetric encoder-decoder with skip connections at every resolution, originally for biomedical segmentation with very few training images. The skip connections preserve high-frequency spatial detail through the bottleneck — the exact reason it became the default backbone for diffusion models (DDPM, Stable Diffusion v1/v2/SDXL) before Diffusion Transformers (DiT, SD3, FLUX) started displacing it at frontier scale. Still the workhorse for dense prediction, medical imaging, and most ControlNet-style adapters.
questions: Why are skip connections at every resolution the structural insight of U-Net — what spatial information would otherwise vanish at the bottleneck?
Walk through the U-Net forward pass for a 512×512 input — where does the parameter and FLOP budget actually sit?
Why did U-Net dominate medical-image segmentation despite tiny training sets — what does the architecture's prior buy you?
How does Stable Diffusion's U-Net differ from the original — added attention blocks, time embedding, cross-attention conditioning?
Why is the U-Net's bottleneck a natural place to inject text conditioning via cross-attention?
What does DiT (Diffusion Transformer, SD3, Sora) replace U-Net with, and why does it scale better past ~1B parameters?
How do residual + attention blocks inside a U-Net level interact with the cross-resolution skip — what gets propagated and what gets erased?
Why does ControlNet copy the encoder half of the U-Net and leave the decoder frozen — what's the gradient-flow argument?
How would you size the depth (number of downsampling steps) of a U-Net for a 1024×1024 image vs a 4K image?
Why is the original U-Net's transposed convolution often replaced by bilinear upsample + conv — what artifact does the latter avoid?
How does a 3D U-Net (medical, video diffusion) trade compute against capacity in the temporal dimension?
Why is U-Net inference compute-bound at 1024² but memory-bandwidth-bound at 256²?
What does the latent-space U-Net (SD's 8× downsampled latent) buy in compute, and what does it cost in fidelity?
How does INT8 / FP8 quantization break U-Net residual blocks differently from Transformer FFNs?
Why is the FID gap between a well-tuned U-Net and a same-FLOP DiT shrinking and reversing past a certain scale?
What's the correct way to stitch a ControlNet conditioning into a fine-tuned LoRA U-Net at inference?
How would you debug a U-Net where the generated images have correct semantics but mottled, low-frequency artifacts?
Is U-Net obsolete at frontier scale, or is its inductive bias permanently useful for low-data and dense-prediction regimes?

vit.blurb: Dosovitskiy et al 2020: chop an image into 16×16 patches, project them, prepend a [CLS] token, run a vanilla Transformer. With enough data (JFT-300M+) it matches or beats the best CNNs while being more compute-efficient at scale. The Transformer-on-images design that opened the door to multimodal foundation models (CLIP, BLIP, LLaVA), the Diffusion Transformer (DiT, SD3, Sora), and the modern thesis that 'one architecture, one optimizer, one tokenizer' should handle everything.
questions: Why does ViT need 100M+ images to outperform a ResNet — what's the inductive-bias-vs-data tradeoff?
Walk through patch embedding — why is a strided conv mathematically equivalent and what does it lose / preserve?
Why does the [CLS] token convention persist when global average pooling sometimes works as well?
How does patch size (8 vs 16 vs 32) trade compute against fine-grained spatial reasoning?
Why does ViT-L/14 specifically dominate CLIP and downstream multimodal pretraining?
What's the inductive-bias deficit of ViT relative to a CNN, and how do hybrids (CoAtNet, Swin) re-introduce some?
Why did Swin's shifted-window attention briefly win dense prediction before being superseded by ViT + DETR-style decoders?
How does positional encoding for ViT (1D learned, 2D sinusoidal, axial) trade off, and does anyone use absolute PE anymore?
What does MAE / DINOv2 self-supervised pretraining buy ViT that supervised ImageNet pretraining did not?
Why is DiT (ViT used as a diffusion backbone) outperforming U-Net at scale — what's the architectural insight?
How does ViT's compute scale with input resolution (4× cost for 2× resolution) and what tricks (token merging, NaViT) reduce it?
Why is ViT's KV cache equivalent (no causal mask, full self-attention) so much smaller per token than a decoder LLM's?
How would you adapt ViT for video — temporal patches, factorized attention, or just stack 3D tokens?
What does the ViT-22B paper teach about whether vision Transformers obey LLM-style scaling laws?
Why is ViT inference latency on edge silicon worse than a comparable CNN, and what does FlashAttention buy you here?
How would you fine-tune a ViT for a 100-class dataset of 5k images — full fine-tune, LoRA, linear probe, or feature reuse?
What's the right way to debug a ViT whose attention maps look like attention sinks, with [CLS] consuming all the mass?
Is ViT fundamental, or did it win because it shared infrastructure with the LLM stack at exactly the right moment?

layer-norm.blurb: Ba, Kiros & Hinton 2016: normalize across the feature dimension per-sample, decoupling the statistics from batch size and making BN's training/inference asymmetry irrelevant. The default in every Transformer (Vaswani 2017) and the reason BN is essentially absent from modern LLMs. RMSNorm (Zhang 2019) further drops the mean centering — used in Llama, Mistral, Gemma, DeepSeek — and the pre-norm vs post-norm choice (Xiong 2020) is one of the few decisions that genuinely matters past 100B parameters.
questions: Why does LayerNorm normalize across features rather than across the batch — what bottleneck of BN does that fix?
What problem does pre-norm Transformer fix that post-norm couldn't, and why does it slightly hurt small-model loss?
Walk through RMSNorm's simplification — drop the mean, drop β — and quantify what it actually saves at 70B scale.
Why is LayerNorm essentially mandatory for sequence-parallel and pipeline-parallel training while BN is hostile?
How does LayerNorm placement interact with residual streams — derive the gradient flow for a post-norm vs pre-norm block.
Why do >100-layer post-norm Transformers diverge in training, and why does Pre-LN or DeepNet's β-init save them?
What does the γ scale parameter actually do at inference, and why does removing it (RMSNorm with γ only, no β) work?
Why is LayerNorm's epsilon the kind of hyperparameter that everyone ignores until FP16 / BF16 training blows up?
How does LayerNorm interact with FP8 / mixed-precision — why does the scaling factor often stay in higher precision?
What's the gradient cost of LayerNorm relative to a matmul, and why does fusing it (xformers, FlashAttention) materially matter?
Why do diffusion U-Nets prefer GroupNorm while Transformers prefer LayerNorm — derive from the activation distribution shape.
How does LayerNorm interact with attention sink (Xiao 2024) — does the normalization amplify or suppress the phenomenon?
What does the Pre-LN Transformer gradient norm look like at depth N, and why does it stay bounded while Post-LN explodes?
Why does removing LayerNorm entirely (NormFormer, ReZero) sometimes work and sometimes catastrophically fail?
How would you debug a 70B model whose loss spikes around a specific layer — is it RMSNorm, the residual, or the FFN?
What's the cost story of LayerNorm in a CUDA kernel — bandwidth-bound, latency-bound, or fusable?
Why does ConvNeXt swap BN for LN in a CNN context, and what does that say about the underlying preference?
Is LayerNorm fundamental to the Transformer's success, or could a careful init + residual scheme remove it entirely?

imagenet.blurb: Deng, Li & Fei-Fei 2009: 14M images across 21k WordNet categories, with the ILSVRC subset (1.28M / 1k classes) becoming the benchmark that organized a decade of vision research. AlexNet's 2012 win on it kicked off deep learning; ResNet's 2015 win on it normalized 100+ layer training; and even in 2026 it is still the linear-probe benchmark for self-supervised vision (DINOv2, MAE, SAM-2 backbones). The dataset, not the architecture, was arguably the actual unlock.
questions: Why is ImageNet's WordNet-derived class hierarchy both a strength (hierarchy) and a known flaw (class overlap, weird granularity)?
How did ILSVRC's standardized 1k subset enable cross-paper comparison in a way that the full 21k set couldn't?
Walk through Top-1 vs Top-5 accuracy — when do they decouple and what does that say about the labels?
Why is the dataset, not AlexNet, often credited as the actual unlock for deep learning?
What documented label biases (gender, geography, dog breeds) exist in ImageNet, and how do they leak into pretrained features?
How would you re-curate ImageNet in 2026 for vision-language pretraining — what would you change, what would you keep?
Why is ImageNet still the linear-probe benchmark for SSL (DINOv2, MAE) when classification-supervised pretraining is mostly dead?
What did ImageNet-A / ImageNet-V2 / ImageNet-Sketch reveal about the limits of test-set accuracy?
How did ImageNet's resolution standard (224×224) shape a decade of architecture choices that ViT and DiT still inherit?
Why is fine-grained classification (dog breeds, bird species) overrepresented in ImageNet, and how does that bias the linear probe?
What does the saturation of ImageNet top-1 accuracy (now >90%) tell us about the metric vs the task?
Why did self-supervised pretraining on uncurated web data (DINOv2's LVD-142M) start outperforming ImageNet-supervised transfer?
How would you build a modern equivalent of ImageNet for video understanding — same template or a different framing?
What is the real-world cost (wall-clock, dollars) of training a ViT-L/14 on ImageNet-21k on H100s in 2026?
Why does ImageNet-pretrained still beat from-scratch on small downstream datasets even now — pure feature-bank effect?
How does ImageNet's static-frame, single-object framing fail for compositional tasks like CLEVR / GQA?
Is ImageNet still useful in 2026, or has its function been entirely subsumed by web-scale image-text contrastive datasets?
What's the lesson from ImageNet for AI infrastructure — the dataset, not the model, was the moat?

decision-trees.blurb: Quinlan's ID3 (1986), C4.5, and Breiman's CART (1984): recursively split the feature space on the best information-gain / Gini-impurity feature until leaves are pure. Standalone trees are weak learners; the breakthrough is ensembles — Random Forest (Breiman 2001) and Gradient Boosted Decision Trees (Friedman 1999). GBDT (XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost) remains the dominant tabular ML approach in 2026, and most production ML before LLMs runs on it.
questions: Walk through the ID3 / C4.5 / CART differences — entropy vs Gini, multi-way vs binary, classification vs regression.
Why does a single decision tree overfit so badly, and what's the bias-variance argument for going to an ensemble?
What does Random Forest's bagging + feature subsampling buy you over a single deep tree?
How does Gradient Boosting differ structurally from Random Forest — sequential vs parallel, residual fitting vs averaging?
Why does GBDT still dominate tabular ML in 2026 despite the deep-learning takeover everywhere else?
Walk through XGBoost's second-order Taylor expansion of the loss — what does using the Hessian buy over Friedman's gradient-only formulation?
How does LightGBM's histogram + leaf-wise growth differ from XGBoost's level-wise — when does each win?
Why is CatBoost's ordered boosting the right answer for high-cardinality categorical features?
How does feature importance from a tree (Gini importance, gain, SHAP) decompose for a global explanation?
What does the 'no free lunch' look like specifically for trees vs neural nets on tabular data — when does a small MLP actually win?
Why is missing-value handling natively trivial in trees but a permanent headache in deep learning?
How would you tune XGBoost vs LightGBM for a Kaggle-style tabular contest in 2026 — same hyperparameters, different defaults?
When does a tree ensemble's predictions stop being calibrated, and what's the standard fix (Platt scaling, isotonic)?
How does TabNet / FT-Transformer / SAINT compare to GBDT on a real production tabular dataset, not the curated benchmarks?
Why is online / streaming learning still a hard story for trees, and what do Hoeffding trees / Mondrian forests buy?
How would you serve an XGBoost model at 100k QPS — Treelite compilation, sparse vs dense features, batch vs single-row?
What's the right way to debug a GBDT model whose validation AUC dropped 5% after a feature-store schema change?
Will trees still be the tabular default in 2030, or will tabular foundation models (TabPFN, V2) finally close the gap?

xgboost.blurb: Chen & Guestrin 2016: GBDT done right — second-order Taylor expansion of the loss, regularized split scoring, sparse-aware split finder, cache-conscious histograms, and weighted quantile sketches. Won most Kaggle tabular competitions for years and remains the production default for fraud detection, ranking, and tabular ML in 2026, alongside LightGBM and CatBoost. The library every ML platform's tabular path quietly depends on.
questions: Walk through XGBoost's regularized objective L = Σ l(y, ŷ) + Ω(f) — what does Ω specifically penalize?
Why does XGBoost's second-order Taylor expansion (using both g and h) beat Friedman's first-order formulation?
How does the sparse-aware split finder work, and why is it the right answer for one-hot encoded features?
Why does histogram binning (used by 'hist' tree method) make XGBoost competitive with LightGBM?
What does the weighted quantile sketch solve in the approximate split-finding regime?
How does XGBoost's column subsampling and row subsampling compose with bagging — does it actually reduce variance like RF?
Walk through the standard hyperparameter sweep — max_depth, eta, min_child_weight, subsample — in priority order for a 1M-row tabular dataset.
Why is early stopping on a validation set effectively a regularizer in GBDT, and how does it interact with eta?
How do XGBoost's monotone constraints / interaction constraints encode domain knowledge into the tree — when does that beat unconstrained?
When does XGBoost beat LightGBM, and when does LightGBM win — exact split vs leaf-wise growth tradeoff?
Why does CatBoost handle high-cardinality categoricals (zip code, user_id) better than XGBoost out of the box?
How does XGBoost's GPU histogram tree method scale across multi-GPU, and where does the all-reduce cost?
What's the right way to deploy a 5000-tree XGBoost model at 100k QPS — Treelite, ONNX Runtime, custom C++?
How does feature importance disagreement between gain, weight, and SHAP indicate model misspecification?
When does an XGBoost model's calibration break, and why is isotonic regression the standard recalibration tool?
Why does dropping or adding a single rare-but-correlated feature sometimes flip XGBoost rankings — what does that say about robustness?
How would you debug an XGBoost model that loses 3 points of AUC after retraining on the same data with a new sklearn version?
Is XGBoost a permanent tabular default, or will TabPFN-style transformer-on-tabular eventually win at >1M rows?

svm.blurb: Cortes & Vapnik 1995: find the maximum-margin separating hyperplane and lift to high-dimensional spaces via the kernel trick (RBF, polynomial, string kernels). Dominated text classification, bioinformatics, and structured prediction in the 1995-2010 window before deep learning ate the high-data tasks. Still alive in low-data regimes (small biomedical, niche industrial), and the structural ancestor of every margin-based / kernelized method — including the implicit-kernel view of attention.
questions: Walk through the maximum-margin hyperplane derivation — why does the dual formulation give you the kernel trick almost for free?
What does the kernel trick actually buy — implicit feature mapping, never having to compute φ(x) explicitly?
Why is the RBF kernel essentially a similarity Gaussian, and how does γ trade off bias and variance?
How does the soft-margin C parameter trade off margin width against misclassification — derive from the slack variables?
Why is SVM training O(N²) to O(N³) in samples — and what does Pegasos / SGD-SVM buy you at scale?
What's the structural risk minimization story behind SVM, and why did it beat ERM-based methods on small data?
Walk through SMO (Sequential Minimal Optimization) — why is the two-variable subproblem closed-form?
How does the kernel trick connect to attention's QK^T similarity — is attention a learned implicit kernel?
Why do SVMs need feature scaling to behave well, while trees don't?
How does multi-class SVM (one-vs-rest, one-vs-one, Crammer-Singer) actually behave in practice on imbalanced classes?
Why did SVM lose to deep learning above ~1M samples but still wins on small biomedical / industrial datasets?
What's the connection between SVM and logistic regression in the limit of soft margin → ∞?
How would you choose a kernel for a structured prediction task — string kernel, graph kernel, learned kernel?
Why is SVM hyperparameter search (C, γ) non-convex over both axes and effectively requires grid + cross-validation?
When would you choose SVM over a small MLP in 2026 — under what specific data regime is it strictly better?
How would you debug an RBF-SVM whose accuracy drops 10% after a feature pipeline change but log-loss stays flat?
What's the right way to serve an SVM at 100k QPS — precompute support vectors, vectorize the kernel evaluation, ONNX?
Is SVM permanently obsoleted by neural nets, or does the kernel-trick framing have a future as the math of implicit attention?

kmeans-clustering.blurb: Lloyd's algorithm (1957, published 1982): alternate between assigning each point to the nearest centroid and recomputing centroids as cluster means, iterating to a local minimum of the within-cluster sum of squares. Still the canonical clustering algorithm — used inside FAISS for IVF index training, vector quantization (VQ-VAE codebook updates), embedding deduplication, and warm-start initialization for more sophisticated methods. The mini-batch variant (Sculley 2010) scales it to billions of vectors.
questions: Walk through Lloyd's algorithm — assignment step, update step — why does it converge to a local minimum but not the global one?
Why is k-means objective (within-cluster SSE) NP-hard in general but Lloyd's still works well in practice?
What does k-means++ initialization actually buy over random — derive the O(log k) approximation guarantee.
How does the choice of k get made in practice — elbow, silhouette, gap statistic, or just 'business k'?
Why does k-means assume spherical, equal-variance clusters — when does that assumption catastrophically fail?
How does k-means relate to Gaussian Mixture Model (EM) — special case with hard assignment and identity covariance?
Walk through mini-batch k-means — why does the variance of centroid updates not blow up convergence?
Why is k-means used inside FAISS IVF for inverted-list assignment — what's the recall/latency tradeoff?
How does VQ-VAE's codebook update reduce to k-means with a straight-through estimator on top?
Why does k-means underperform on high-dimensional data (the curse), and what does PCA + k-means buy?
When does spectral clustering beat k-means specifically — non-convex clusters, manifold structure?
How would you cluster 1B 768-dim embeddings — distributed k-means, FAISS-GPU, hierarchical k-means?
What does the centroid drift look like under streaming data, and why is online k-means subtle?
Why is the choice of distance metric (Euclidean vs cosine) load-bearing — and what does k-medoids buy?
How would you debug a k-means clustering that produces one giant cluster + many singletons?
Why is k-means a poor fit for clustering text embeddings without normalization, and how does spherical k-means fix that?
When deduplicating a 100M-image dataset, would you use k-means + nearest-centroid, LSH, or HNSW threshold? Tradeoffs?
Is k-means permanent in the ML toolkit, or do learned hashing / contrastive clustering retire it for embeddings?

pca.blurb: Pearson 1901; Hotelling 1933: project data onto the orthogonal directions of maximum variance via the eigendecomposition of the covariance matrix, equivalently the SVD of the centered data matrix. Still the workhorse linear dimensionality reducer, the reference for embedding visualization (alongside t-SNE / UMAP), and the conceptual ancestor of every low-rank decomposition that matters — LoRA's ΔW = BA, embedding compression, Matryoshka representations, randomized SVD inside FAISS-PQ training.
questions: Walk through PCA's two equivalent derivations — variance maximization vs reconstruction error minimization.
Why is centering the data essential, and what does scaling (z-score) change about the resulting principal components?
How does PCA reduce to SVD on the centered data matrix — and which is faster to compute in practice?
What's the relationship between PCA and the eigendecomposition of the empirical covariance matrix?
Why does randomized SVD (Halko 2011) make PCA scale to billion-row data — what's the approximation guarantee?
How does PCA whitening differ from PCA projection, and when do you want whitening (CCA, OOD detection)?
Why is PCA optimal under Gaussian assumptions but suboptimal for heavy-tailed distributions — what does sparse PCA buy?
How does PCA relate to LoRA's low-rank decomposition — same algebra, different fit objective?
Walk through choosing the rank k — elbow on explained variance, eigengap, downstream task accuracy?
Why does PCA on contextual embeddings (BERT, CLIP) reveal anisotropy, and how does whitening / BERT-flow fix it?
How does kernel PCA generalize linear PCA — and why has it largely been displaced by autoencoders?
When does PCA-projection-as-feature-extraction beat learned encoders on small data?
How would you compress a 1B-row × 768-dim embedding store via PCA — at what rank does retrieval recall actually drop?
Why is incremental / online PCA non-trivial, and what does Oja's rule give you?
What's the connection between PCA and Matryoshka representation learning — implicit nested low-rank?
How does PCA interact with batch normalization at inference — why does it sometimes silently break?
How would you debug a downstream classifier whose accuracy collapses after PCA preprocessing — too few components or rotation issue?
Is PCA permanent or being slowly retired — what gap do learned compressors (autoencoders, VAEs) actually fill?

alphago.blurb: Silver et al 2016 (DeepMind): MCTS guided by a deep policy network and a deep value network, trained first on human games (SL), then via self-play RL. Beat Lee Sedol 4-1 in March 2016, then AlphaGo Zero (2017) removed human data entirely and exceeded all prior versions in 40 days of self-play. The 'search + neural net' template (search guides exploration, networks compress evaluation) is the ancestor of every modern reasoning model — o1, R1, o3 — that pairs RL with chain-of-thought search.
questions: Walk through MCTS's four phases — selection, expansion, simulation, backpropagation — and how the PUCT formula balances exploration vs exploitation.
Why does AlphaGo's policy network bias the tree search at the selection step, and what failure mode does that fix?
What does the value network replace in classical MCTS — and why does it cut the rollout depth so dramatically?
How does AlphaGo Zero remove the SL bootstrap entirely — what's the role of self-play in stabilizing the policy?
Why is the PUCT exploration constant a load-bearing hyperparameter, and how does it interact with the policy prior?
Walk through AlphaZero's generalization to chess and shogi — what assumptions about Go did it actually rely on?
What's the connection between AlphaGo's 'search + nets' template and o1-style reasoning models — RL with verifiable rewards?
How does MuZero remove the assumption of a known environment model — and why is that a separate breakthrough from AlphaZero?
Why is self-play data-efficient compared to playing against a fixed opponent — what does the curriculum buy?
How would you parallelize MCTS across thousands of CPU/GPU workers without breaking the tree's statistics?
What's the entropy-collapse problem in self-play training, and how does AlphaZero's noise injection at the root prevent it?
Why does AlphaGo's value network underperform on positions far from the self-play distribution — and what does that imply for OOD reasoning models?
How does the temperature-annealing schedule on MCTS visit-counts shape exploration in early vs late training?
Why is the 'amateur dan' to 'superhuman' jump in self-play not smooth — what does the rating curve actually look like?
How does the search-RL paradigm scale to language reasoning — what's the search space, and how do you evaluate a 'leaf'?
Why is reward hacking different in AlphaGo (no humans) vs RLHF / o1 (humans + verifier)?
When deploying an AlphaGo-style system, what's the wall-clock cost of MCTS at inference vs running just the policy network?
Is search-guided RL the next general paradigm for capability gain, or did AlphaGo just exploit Go's clean game-tree structure?

alphafold.blurb: Jumper et al 2021 (DeepMind): AlphaFold 2 brought protein structure prediction to near-experimental accuracy on CASP14 — RMSD ~1Å on 2/3 of targets. The architecture (Evoformer with MSA + pair representation, Structure Module with invariant point attention, end-to-end differentiable) replaced 50 years of physics-based modeling. AlphaFold 3 (2024) generalized to protein-ligand and protein-nucleic-acid complexes via diffusion. The reference case for 'an ML system has solved a fundamental science problem'.
questions: Walk through AlphaFold 2's high-level architecture — MSA → Evoformer → Structure Module → 3D coordinates.
Why is the multiple sequence alignment (MSA) the key input — what coevolutionary signal does it carry?
What does the pair representation actually encode, and why is updating it the core of Evoformer?
How does invariant point attention preserve SE(3) equivariance — what would naive attention break?
Why is the structure module's recycling (3 iterations) load-bearing — what does each pass refine?
How does the FAPE (Frame Aligned Point Error) loss differ from raw RMSD, and why does it train better?
